coredata.dc:description: The growing demand for efficient water treatment technologies has driven the development of advanced nanofiltration (NF) membranes capable of tackling multiple challenges, including self-cleaning, effective pollutant removal, and precise ion selectivity. Herein, we fabricated a photocatalytic nanofiltration membrane using in situ grown TiO2 on cellulose nanocrystal (CNC) as an interlayer (TiO2/CNC) via vacuum-assisted interfacial polymerization. The TiO2 catalysts, loaded on the CNC surface, effectively degrade organic matter under ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation. The successful incorporating of TiO2/CNC interlayer resulted in a membrane with rough, hydrophilic, and loosely selective polyamide (PA) layer. The as-prepared membrane (TFC@TiO2/CNC) exhibited high water permeance of 23.3 L/m2·h·bar, along with excellent salt rejection (95.5 % for Na2SO4) and organic matter retention (96.4 % for Congo red and 99.1 % for humic acid). In testing involving the separation of organic and salt mixtures, the membrane demonstrated an average 99 % flux recovery after 30 min of UV irradiation, while the membrane without TiO2/CNC interlayer experienced an average flux recovery of 79 %. Following UV irradiation, the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane showed no significant decline in water permeance or retention for both Na2SO4 and humic acid, indicating strong anti-fouling properties with a flux attenuation of 9.2 % compared to the initial cycle which had a flux attenuation of 21.6 %. For dye fouling, the membrane with photocatalysts displayed excellent flux recovery. The results indicated that the TiO2/CNC interlayer-enhanced membrane holds great potential for treatment of organic wastewater in the treatment of industrial wastewater.

head.ce:title: TiO2/CNC interlayer assisted photocatalytic nanofiltration membrane for enhanced self-cleaning performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The growing demand for efficient water treatment technologies has driven the development of advanced nanofiltration (NF) membranes capable of tackling multiple challenges, including self-cleaning, effective pollutant removal, and precise ion selectivity. Herein, we fabricated a photocatalytic nanofiltration membrane using in situ grown TiO2 on cellulose nanocrystal (CNC) as an interlayer (TiO2/CNC) via vacuum-assisted interfacial polymerization. The TiO2 catalysts, loaded on the CNC surface, effectively degrade organic matter under ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation. The successful incorporating of TiO2/CNC interlayer resulted in a membrane with rough, hydrophilic, and loosely selective polyamide (PA) layer. The as-prepared membrane (TFC@TiO2/CNC) exhibited high water permeance of 23.3 L/m2·h·bar, along with excellent salt rejection (95.5 % for Na2SO4) and organic matter retention (96.4 % for Congo red and 99.1 % for humic acid). In testing involving the separation of organic and salt mixtures, the membrane demonstrated an average 99 % flux recovery after 30 min of UV irradiation, while the membrane without TiO2/CNC interlayer experienced an average flux recovery of 79 %. Following UV irradiation, the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane showed no significant decline in water permeance or retention for both Na2SO4 and humic acid, indicating strong anti-fouling properties with a flux attenuation of 9.2 % compared to the initial cycle which had a flux attenuation of 21.6 %. For dye fouling, the membrane with photocatalysts displayed excellent flux recovery. The results indicated that the TiO2/CNC interlayer-enhanced membrane holds great potential for treatment of organic wastewater in the treatment of industrial wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution, driven by the release of organic contaminants, synthetic dyes, and high salinity, has become an increasingly urgent issue for both environmental protection and industrial operations [1–4]. These pollutants not only pose significant risks to human health and ecosystems but also create complex challenges in wastewater treatment and resource recovery [4–7]. Among the advanced technologies developed to address these issues, nanofiltration (NF) membranes have gained widespread attention for their ability to selectively remove contaminants while maintaining high water permeability [8–11]. NF membranes are particularly effective in the separation of salts, organic molecules, and dyes from aqueous solutions, making them valuable for applications in industrial wastewater treatment, water recycling, and desalination processes [12–17].

Despite their advantages, traditional NF membranes face several limitations that hinder their long-term performance. Membrane fouling, caused by the accumulation of organic matter, microorganisms, and other pollutants on the membrane surface, is a persistent challenge [18–22]. Fouling reduces water flux, leading to increased operational costs due to frequent cleaning and maintenance [21,23–30]. Furthermore, conventional NF membranes are typically passive in nature, meaning they could only filter contaminants but lack the ability to actively degrade or transform harmful organic compounds. This limitation reduces their effectiveness in treating complex wastewater streams containing persistent organic pollutants [20–22,31–33].

To overcome these challenges, there is a growing demand for the development of advanced multifunctional membranes that exhibit enhanced self-cleaning, antifouling, and pollutant degradation properties [34–39]. Such membranes could maintain high permeability while preventing fouling and simultaneously breaking down organic contaminants. By incorporating catalytic and hydrophilic materials into membrane structures, researchers aim to create next-generation NF membranes that not only filter out pollutants but also actively degrade them, thereby extending the operational life of the membrane and improving overall water treatment efficiency [13,34–45].

Photocatalytic membranes are a typical type of self-cleaning membrane which represent an innovative approach to water treatment, combining the advantages of membrane filtration with the catalytic properties of photocatalytic materials [44,46–49]. These membranes are designed to simultaneously filter water and degrade organic pollutants or microbes under the influence of light, typically ultraviolet (UV) irradiation [46,47,50]. When the membrane is illuminated by light in the appropriate wavelength range, the photocatalytic reaction is activated, and the absorption of the photocatalyst excites the electrons from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), resulting in the positive charge left in the VB and triggering the photocatalytic reaction [11,51]. For example, Shao et al. prepared a dual-function polyamide NF membrane with photocatalytic self-cleaning performance by incorporating carbon quantum dots during interfacial polymerization [52]. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is a highly efficient photocatalytic material widely used in catalytic membrane applications due to its unique physical and chemical properties [11,48,53,54]. To enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2, researchers have focused on improving its light absorption and quantum efficiency by doping with metal ions and combining it with other materials [54,55]. Cellulose nanocrystal (CNC) is commonly used as a template for preparing mesoporous nanomaterials due to its small mesh size, good dispersibility, and large specific surface area [56]. Lee et al. used CNC as a template for growing TiO2, producing TiO2 materials with enhanced photocatalytic and adsorption properties that exhibit excellent dye degradation effects [57]. Li et al. grew TiO2 in situ on CNC for the reduction of hexavalent chromium, forming ligand–metal charge transfer (LMCT) complexes with TiO2, which enhanced visible light absorption of TiO2 and photodegradation efficiency [58]. Mi et al. prepared interfacially polymerized polyamide nanofiltration (NF) membranes with self-cleaning ability using a combination of carbon quantum dots/titanium dioxide (CQD/TiO2) nanoparticles (NPs). In contrast to cyclic physical water cleaning, the flux recovery was maintained at 97–98 % during the cyclic contamination-irradiation process, demonstrating stable and efficient self-cleaning performance [53]. This self-cleaning mechanism helps to mitigate membrane fouling and prolong membrane lifespan, resulting in more reliable and cost-effective water treatment processes [44,49,59–61].

However, thin-film composite membranes commonly used in nanofiltration have a selective layer that is only tens to hundreds of nanometers thick, making it difficult to effectively combine with catalyst materials. Therefore, incorporating as much catalyst as possible into the nanolayer is a crucial factor limiting the development of catalytic nanofiltration membranes [53,62,63]. Traditionally, incorporating nanoparticles directly during the interfacial polymerization process results in limited doping. Introducing nanoparticles through the formation of an intermediate layer in the nanofiltration membrane can enhance the stability of the nanoparticles within the membrane and significantly increase their concentration [46–50,62–64]. Hou et al. evaluated the construction of a nanoparticle-based interlayer, demonstrating that the interlayer of Noria and 2,2′-benzidinedisulfonic acid nanoparticles facilitated the formation of a thin, crumpled polyamide layer. This structure reduced hydraulic resistance and increased the effective area during the separation process [65].

In this paper, we positioned nano TiO2 photocatalysts onto cellulose nanocrystal (CNC), effectively immobilizing the catalysts to prevent any loss. The TiO2-loaded CNC were deposited onto the substrate by vacuum filtration, creating a uniform interlayer (TiO2/CNC) for interfacial polymerization. Subsequently, a thin film composite membrane was formed upon the TiO2/CNC layer as shown in Fig. 1 . Despite the catalysts being incorporated into the interlayer, the catalytic degradation effects were highly effective. Additionally, the selective layer was minimally influenced by the interlayer, exhibiting similar separation performance and improved water permeation. The self-cleaning effect of the membrane was validated by filtering humic acid and dye solutions, with the flux almost fully recovering after 30 min of UV irradiation. This indicates its potential for application in the treatment of high salinity wastewater in the industry.

Polyether sulfone (PES) ultrafiltration membrane (NADIR®UP020) with a molecular weight cut-off of 20,000 Da, serving as the substrate for NF membranes, was pruchased from Jienuo Environmental Technology Co., Ltd., China. CNC were obtained from InnoTech Alberta in Canada, the sulfur content of CNC was 0.831 wt%. Piperazine (PIP, >99.5 %), 1,3,5-benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC, >98 %), Congo red (CR), methylene blue (MB), methyl blue (MYB), ethanol (95 %), polyethylene glycol (PEG, >99 %), n-hexane (97 %) were analytical grade and purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Humic acid (HA) was obtained from Yuanye Bio-Technology Corporation (Shanghai, China). Titanium (IV) oxysulfate solution (TiOSO4·H2SO4·H2O, ∼15 wt% in dilute sulfuric acid) used as the titanium source was pruchased from Aldrich. (Shanghai, China). Sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), magnesium sulfate anhydrous (MgSO4), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride anhydrous (MgCl2) and calcium chloride (CaCl2) were analytical grade and purchased from Fortune Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of TiO2 loaded CNC and TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: TiO2-loaded CNC was synthesized using a previously described hydrothermal method [66]. Initially, 0.2 g of CNC was ultrasonicated in 200 mL of ultrapure water until a uniform mixture was achieved. Subsequently, 4 mL of TiOSO4 was added, and the solution was stirred at 70 °C for 2 h. After cooling the reaction mixture to room temperature, the product was extracted by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 6 min. The supernatant was discarded, and the resulting suspension was collected and dried in an oven to yield TiO2/CNC solid powder. The obtained TiO2/CNC was then diluted with ultrapure water to a certain concentration and subjected to ultrasonic treatment until a homogeneous mixture was formed. TiO2 particles were prepared using the same method without the addition of CNC, serving as a control.

The TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane was fabricated via a vacuum filtration-assisted interfacial polymerization process as shown in Fig. 1. The as-prepared TiO2/CNC suspension, prepared at varying concentrations, was mixed with PIP aqueous solution and introduced into a vacuum filtration device containing a PES supporting medium. As the nanoparticles uniformly deposited on the substrate, with no obvious water droplets on the surface of the TiO2/CNC layer, a TMC organic solution was poured into the filter funnel for a certain time as reaction time (RT). Throughout the polymerization process, the vacuum pump operated continuously to maintain interlayer stability. The reaction was terminated by discarding the organic solution and rinsing the membrane surface several times with n-hexane. Finally, the membrane was transferred in an oven at 70°C for 7 min. TFC membranes were prepared using the same IP process, but without the incorporation of an interlayer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of TiO2/CNC and NF membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The nanoparticles and membrane morphologies were characterized using a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-7800, Jeol, Japan). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was employed to investigate the surface roughness of the membranes. The wettability and zeta potential of the membranes were measured using a contact angle measuring system (DM-701, Kyowa Interface Science Co., Ltd., Japan) and a surPASS electrokinetic analyzer (Anton Parr GmbH, Austria). The chemical compositions of the nanoparticles and membranes were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS, Jeol, Japan), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo, MA, USA). UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) was conducted using a spectrophotometer (Varian Cary 5000, USA) with an integrating sphere accessory, using BaSO4 as the reference substance. In this experiment, samples of TiO2 and TiO2/CNC nanocomposites with adsorbed HA (designated as TiO2-HA and TiO2/CNC-HA) were prepared. Appropriate amounts of TiO2 or TiO2/CNC powder samples were added to a 20 mg/L HA aqueous solution under stirring, and the adsorption process was carried out overnight in the dark. The suspension was then filtered, and the collected solids were dried at 60 °C. DRS measurements were obtained for TiO2-HA, TiO2/CNC-HA, TiO2/CNC, and TiO2. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the solid samples were measured on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 230 nm. Molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of membranes were explored by filtrating 100 mg/L PEG aqueous solutions at 0.3 MPa. The rejection (R TOC) for PEG was calculated according to equation (1) [67]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of HA by TiO2/CNC
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 g/L TiO2/CNC or TiO2 powder was added to 20 mg/L humic acid solution and mixed thoroughly. Then, the container was placed in a UV dark box for illumination. Periodically, samples were taken, centrifuged to obtain the supernatant, and tested for degradation efficiency. The concentration of humic acid was measured at 254 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The same procedure was performed without TiO2/CNC to assess the photocatalytic effect. To evaluate the effect of light, the same solution was placed in a dark box without illumination to compare the adsorption effect of the photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation tests of TFC and TFC@TiO2/CNC membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The nanofiltration performance of membranes were conducted by a flat sheet membrane setup with cross-flow mode and effective membrane area of 22.1 cm2 as shown in Fig. S1. Pure water and different salt solution (2000 mg/L) were carried out as feed solutions for testing the water permeance and salt rejection under 0.6 MPa and flow rate of 40 mL/min at room temperature. The system was stabilized for 1 h before each data collecting in 30 min. Permeance and salt rejection (R, %) were evaluated by equation (4–5) [70]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-fouling and self-cleaning performance of TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane
ce:section.ce:para:
The anti-fouling performance of the membranes was tested by performing filtration tests on HA (100 mg·L-1) and Na2SO4 (2 g·L-1) mixture solution or CR (1 g·L-1) solution. First, the test membranes were pre-pressurized with ultrapure water for 2 h. Following this, the membranes were used to filter a prepared feed solution at 0.3 MPa and room temperature. During the initial experimental cycle, the membranes continuously filtered the mixed solution of HA and sodium sulfate for 45 h or CR solution for 24 h, with flux and retention data recorded at regular intervals. After this period, the membrane was removed from the test apparatus and rinsed with 2 L of ultrapure water at a consistent rate for 30 min to ensure no further color change occurred on the membrane surface. A ZF-20D dark box UV analyzer equipped with an 8 W UV lamp (wavelength 365 nm) was then used as the UV source. The UV lamp was positioned 10 cm above the membrane surface inside the analyzer, providing a light intensity of 0.363 W/cm2 at this distance. Subsequently, the membranes were reinserted into the test apparatus to evaluate their anti-fouling performance for the second cycle. The same procedure was repeated 3 times. Membrane fouling tendency was assessed using the flux recovery rate (FRR), as calculated by equation (7) [71]:
(7)

where J w,0 is the initial water permeate flux, and J w,1 is the permeate flux after the cleaning of the membrane surface. To provide a more comprehensive assessment of membrane fouling resistance, the total contamination ratio (R t), reversible contamination (R r), and irreversible contamination (R ir) were calculated using equations (8–10):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and photocatalytic performance of TiO2/CNC
ce:section.ce:para: TiO2/CNC were synthesized by solvent-thermal method based on previous literatures [66]. Fig. 2 a shows that the CNC loaded with TiO2 is evenly dispersed, forming a fiber network-like interdeposition. The TEM image (Fig. 2b) provides a more intuitive view, revealing that the TiO2/CNC composite takes the form of slender fibers. XRD analysis indicates the presence of diffraction peaks at 2θ = 25.3°, 37.8°, 47.8°, 54.1°, 62.5°, 69.2°, and 74.7°, corresponding to the [101], [004], [200], [105], [204], [220], and [215] crystal planes of TiO2 anatase, respectively. Fig. 2d compares the degradation efficiency of TiO2/CNC with or without UV irradiation. The removal efficiency reached 80 % in 15 min for TiO2/CNC under the UV irradiation, while nearly 40 % of the HA was removed by TiO2/CNC for 120 min in the dark, primarily due to adsorption. Isolated UV irradiation had no effect on the removal of HA. The comparative experimental data indicated the significant photocatalytic effect of TiO2 on the degradation of organic matters. Comparing TiO2 particles with TiO2/CNC, it is evident that TiO2 alone can quickly reduce HA concentration in a short time, but its larger particle size (Fig. S2) and lower catalytic activity cause the HA concentration to stabilize at around 70 %. This indicates that the catalytic efficiency of TiO2 is lower than that of TiO2/CNC. To investigate whether the PA separation layer would affect the catalytic performance of TiO2/CNC in the membrane, PIP and TiO2/CNC was added to the dye solution, and a small amount of TMC in n-hexane was poured onto the solution surface until the solvent evaporated completely, forming a PA layer on the aqueous phase. The dye solution with the PA separation layer was then exposed to UV light for 2 h, resulting in a significant fading of the dye color (Fig. S3). Notably, the PA layer in this part was much thicker than that of the membrane prepared in the experiment. This indicates that the nanofiltration membrane skin layer does not fully inhibit the photocatalytic performance of TiO2/CNC.

Furthermore, UV–Vis DRS and PL analyses were conducted to demonstrate the enhanced catalytic performance of TiO2/CNC compared to TiO2. The interaction between TiO2 and CNC was studied using UV–vis DRS (Fig. 2e). Results show that the spectra of pure TiO2 and TiO2/CNC samples are similar, with TiO2/CNC displaying a slight increase in absorbance, indicating an improved capacity to absorb light-excited electron/hole pairs. This enhancement is attributed to the formation of a ligand–metal charge transfer (LMCT) complex between electron-rich CNC and TiO2. In this LMCT complex, electrons are transferred to the adsorbed HA, thereby increasing the reduction efficiency. Consequently, the spectrum of TiO2/CNC-HA exhibits a significant red shift, indicating that the presence of HA synergistically enhances light absorption. While HA also has a synergistic effect on TiO2, the absorbance increase is notably smaller. Fig. 2f presents the PL spectra of pure TiO2 and TiO2/CNC. The PL intensity of TiO2 is notably high, indicating a high recombination probability of photogenerated carriers. In contrast, TiO2/CNC shows a much lower PL intensity, suggesting a significant reduction in the radiative recombination probability of self-trapped excitons. This indicates that CNC greatly enhances the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, likely due to its lower charge transfer resistance. The detailed degradation mechanism was depicted in Fig. 1 as UV light strikes the surface of TiO2/CNC, exciting electrons transfer directly from the ground state of adsorbates (CNC) to the conduction band (CB) of TiO2, promoting the efficient separation of charge carriers as evidenced by PL and UV–vis DRS analysis. The injected electrons excited through LMCT are then delivered to HA or the water molecules adsorbed on the surface of TiO2/CNC, generating reactive oxygen species, such as hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anions. These reactive oxygen species then react with organic molecules, breaking them down into harmless substances [50,72]. The TiO2/CNC composite has more efficient light absorption and electron-hole pair charge transfer ability, which makes TiO2/CNC has strong photoreduction activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical composition of TFC and TFC@TiO2/CNC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The surface and cross-sectional structures of the membranes were characterized by SEM. As shown in Fig. 3 , the surface of the TFC membrane was uniform and smooth. In contrast, the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane exhibited noticeable round bulges formed by aggregated nanoparticles. These convex regions are likely exposed TiO2 nanoparticles, which facilitate photocatalysis during the post-filtration cleaning process. The thickness of the selective layer changed from 100 nm to 150 nm with the introduction of the TiO2/CNC interlayer, which measured 136 nm in thickness (Fig. S4b2). As shown in Fig. S4, the TiO2/CNC interlayer forms a distinct layer on the substrate with a thickness ranging from 62 to 441 nm, depending on the TiO2/CNC concentration. The EDS images in Fig. S5 clearly indicate that the TiO2/CNC created an intermediate layer. During the interfacial polymerization reaction, the reactive monomer PIP diffuses from the intermediate layer to the oil phase, forming a PA separation layer on the surface of the intermediate layer. Both TiO2 and CNC in the intermediate layer can form hydrogen bonds with the aqueous-phase monomer PIP, slowing the diffusion of the amine monomer and resulting in a thinner PA layer, which aids in promoting water flux. The surface roughness of the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane increased to 12.1 nm, compared to 6.34 nm for the conventional TFC membrane, as shown in Fig. 3c and 3f. This observation aligns with the surface morphology. Surface roughness influences wetting behavior and anti-fouling properties; increased roughness tends to improve membrane hydrophilicity but can also lead to the accumulation of pollutants, which has an adverse effect on membrane fouling.

The chemical composition of the membrane surface was analyzed using FTIR and XPS. In Fig. 4 a, typical absorption peaks at 1650 and 1580 cm−1, attributed to –CO- and –CO-NH- groups, were observed in both TFC and TFC@TiO2/CNC membranes. These characteristic peaks confirm the successful fabrication of the polyamide selective layer. Upon enlarging the FTIR spectra in the 600–800 cm−1 range (Fig. 4b), peaks at 620 and 629 cm−1 corresponding to Ti-O bonds were evident in the spectrum of the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane, indicating the incorporation of TiO2 nanoparticles into the PA matrix. XPS characterization was further performed to examine the chemical states of carbon (C 1 s), nitrogen (N 1 s), oxygen (O 1 s), and titanium (Ti 2p) in the TFC and TFC@TiO2/CNC membranes. The mass percentages of each element are presented in Table 1 , with the corresponding spectra provided in Fig. S6 of the supplementary materials. Fig. 4c&d confirm the successful incorporation of TiO2 into the PA membrane, as the presence of Ti-O bonds and Ti elements was detected through XPS and EDS spectra. The O/N ratio indicates the degree of crosslinking in the PA membrane, with values closer to 1 reflecting a higher degree of crosslinking, which enhances membrane selectivity. The O/N ratio of the TFC membrane, as shown in Table 1, is 1.43, lower than that of the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane (1.51), indicating a lower degree of cross-linking, consistent with the calculated results. This suggests that the introduction of TiO2 intermediate layer reduces the crosslinking degree of the PA layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface properties and pore size distribution of TFC and TFC@TiO2/CNC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The surface properties, i.e., wettability, pore size distribution and zeta potential were investigated, as shown in Fig. 5 . It is well-known that TiO2 and CNC are hydrophilic materials, which enhance the wetting behavior of the membrane. From the static water contact angle data, the TFC membrane exhibited a contact angle of 44.4°, whereas the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane achieved a water contact angle of 24.7°, attributed to the presence of hydrophilic TiO2 and CNC as shown in Fig. 5c. The improved wetting behavior towards water can also be attributed to the looser polyamide (PA) layer, as the mean pore size of TFC@TiO2/CNC was slightly larger than that of TFC (Fig. 5a&b). The molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of TFC and TFC@TiO2/CNC membranes was 378 Da and 687 Da, respectively. It is noted that the TiO2/CNC interlayer may create some obstacles during interfacial polymerization. Therefore, the physical hindrance by the TiO2/CNC layer resulted in the looser and larger pore size of the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane, which is consistent with the O/N ratio presented in Table 1. The zeta potential results in Fig. 5d showed only slight differences between the TFC and TFC@TiO2/CNC membranes at neutral pH. The TiO2, with its abundant hydroxyl groups, exhibited a stronger negative charge at pH = 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NF separation performance and stability of TFC@TiO2/CNC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Nanofiltration membranes are extensively used for separating monovalent and divalent salts, but traditional membranes often encounter organic contamination during the separation process. This study enhances the anti-fouling and self-cleaning properties of nanofiltration membranes while maintaining their original separation capacity. In this study, a TiO2/CNC mid-layer acted as a photocatalyst to impart self-cleaning ability, but the basic performance in salt separation is essential to meet the requirements of wastewater treatment. Pure water permeance and rejection of Na2SO4 and NaCl were evaluated and are depicted in Fig. 6 a. TiO2/CNC suspension concentration ranging from 0.05 to 0.2 g/L, showed relatively stable nanofiltration performance with ultrahigh water permeability (more than 20 L/m2·h·bar), over 90 % rejection of Na2SO4, and below 20 % rejection of NaCl. As shown in Fig. 6a, the water permeability improves significantly after the introduction of the TiO2/CNC intermediate layer, increasing from 15 L/m2·h·bar to a peak of 23.3 L/m2·h·bar. This improvement is attributed to the hydrophilic nature of the intermediate layer, which significantly reduces the mass transfer resistance in the water channels (Fig. 5c). However, when the concentration of the TiO2/CNC intermediate layer becomes too high, it forms an excessively thick layer (Fig. S4), leading to smaller mass transfer pores and increased resistance, which in turn reduces permeability. Additionally, TiO2/CNC, with its abundant hydroxyl groups, was prone to adsorb PIP molecules, which aggregated amine reactants at the interface, forming a dense and thick polyamide layer that further reduced water permeability. Therefore, when the TiO2/CNC concentration exceeds 0.3 g/L, water flux begins to decrease. Previous chemical structure and pore size analyses indicate that the TiO2/CNC intermediate layer (0.08 g/L) results in a lower degree of cross-linking and a looser pore structure in the PA layer. Consequently, the rejection rate of Na2SO4 decreases slightly, reducing from 98 % to 94 % (Fig. 6b).

Subsequently, the impact of PIP and TMC concentration on salt separation and permeability performance was investigated based on 0.08 g/L TiO2/CNC intermediate layer (Fig. S7&S8). The concentration of reactive monomers had a significant effect on the polyamide structure. Interestingly, the TMC concentration had minimal influence on salt rejection but did affect permeance. The highest water permeance, reaching 15.5 L/m2·h·bar, was achieved with a TMC concentration of 0.15 wt%. Notably, PIP had a profound impact on nanofiltration performance. At low PIP concentrations (below 0.1 wt%), water permeance was exceptionally high, ranging from 23.3 to 29.2 L/m2·h·bar, with an 95.5 % and 88.3 % rejection rate for Na2SO4. As the PIP content increased, rejection rates for Na2SO4 and NaCl also increased, while the flux remained stable at 16.6 L/m2·h·bar. Reaction time followed a normal trend, with longer reaction times leading to reduced flux as shown in Fig. S9. Consequently, the optimal preparation conditions for the NF membrane were identified as 0.15 wt% TMC, 0.05 wt% PIP, and a 1-minute reaction time.

The as-prepared membrane was employed to separate dye molecules, namely Congo red (CR), methyl blue (MYB), and methylene blue (MB). The TFC and TFC@TiO2/CNC membranes exhibited similar retention for CR and MYB, with rejections 96.4 % for CR, and 95.8 % for MYB, respectively (Fig. 7 a). The rejection for MB was 52.1 % for TFC and 67.6 % for TFC@TiO2/CNC, as the molecular weight of MB is smaller than the MWCO of both membranes. However, due to the strong adsorption capability of the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane, the rejection of MB was higher with the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane. The flux of the dye solutions showed slight differences, with a permeance of 28.5 L/m2·h·bar for CR and around 32.2 L/m2·h·bar for both MYB and MB for the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane at 6 bar trans-membrane pressure (Fig. 7b). The TFC membrane exhibited similar rejection but with lower permeation rates. The incorporation of TiO2 in the nanofiltration membrane demonstrates great potential for treating textile wastewater.

Due to the strong negative charge on its surface (Fig. 5d), the membrane effectively separates cations and anions. The rejection of different salts varies significantly based on their charge intensity. Na2SO4, with a dianion, was almost completely rejected by the membrane. The rejection of MgSO4, which has both a dianion and divalent cation, was 66.9 % due to the smaller hydration radius of magnesium ions compared to the sum of the hydration radii of two sodium ions (Fig. 7c). Additionally, cations were prone to penetrate the membrane due to electrostatic forces. Other salts with monovalent anions showed low retention by the membrane, indicating its good ability to separate divalent cations and anions. The membrane prepared with a TiO2/CNC intermediate layer maintained a stable flux over 72 h of continuous filtration of a salt solution, with a retention rate consistently above 94 % as depicted in Fig. 7d. This indicates that the nanofiltration membrane, produced by introducing the TiO2/CNC intermediate layer, ensures long-term stability. The intermediate layer structure remains stable, and the skin layer can also withstand extended filtration tests while maintaining excellent stability.

The above results demonstrate that the incorporation of a TiO2/CNC interlayer had no negative impact on the nanofiltration performance. Furthermore, the self-cleaning property was examined by filtering mixtures of humic acid (HA) and Na2SO4. The feed solution contained 100 mg/L HA and 2 g/L Na2SO4. The tests were conducted in cross-flow mode with a tran-smembrane pressure of 3 bar. The permeate mass was collected to calculate permeation and rejection rates. After a period of filtration, the membrane was removed and the surface of the membrane was cleaned with 2 L ultrapure water for 30 min. Subsequently, the membrane underwent UV irradiation to degrade organic pollutant. The membrane was then returned to the nanofiltration module, and the test was restarted. This cycle was repeated three times to investigate flux recovery and separation performance.

The normalized flux and rejection rates for HA and Na2SO4 were depicted in Fig. 8 . The water flux dropped significantly during the first cycle for both TFC and TFC@TiO2/CNC membranes. After 45 h filtration, the flux of TFC membrane decayed to 70.2 %, and TFC@TiO2/CNC flux attenuation by 78.4 %. After 30 min of UV irradiation, the nanofiltration membrane doped with TiO2 showed a significant self-cleaning property with the permeation flux restored to 100 %, while the flux of the nanofiltration membrane without TiO2 doping only recovered by 80.5 %. In the second circle of filtration experiment, both membranes showed relatively stable permeation flux. For TFC membranes, there is a certain amount of irreversible pollution on the surface, which forms a certain protective layer to prevent further fouling of the membrane surface, so the attenuation of flux is not significant (Fig. 8a). By being activated by UV light, TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane surface showed strong anti-fouling property to prevent the aggregation of pollutants on the membrane surface, resulting in a significant decrease in membrane flux attenuation (9.2 %) as shown in Fig. 8b. In the third cycle, it was found that TiO2 membrane stably achieved anti-fouling effect, while TFC membrane rapidly decreases after a brief flux recovery, reaching a stable state around 70 % of the initial flux. During this period, both membranes maintained stable retention rates for pollutants and divalent anions, indicating their integrity and resistance to UV light exposure. The effect of illumination time on the self-cleaning effect of the membrane was also investigated as shown in Fig. S10. It was found that, after 10 min of UV illumination, the membrane flux did not recover and instead declined more rapidly in the following cycle. After 20 min of illumination, the flux began to recover in the second cycle and nearly fully recovered by the third cycle. This indicates that at least 30 min of illumination is required to effectively remove membrane contamination.

When testing the anti-fouling and self-cleaning performance of the membrane using a dye solution, it was found that the TFC membrane was easily fouled by the dye. After 24 h of filtration, the flux rapidly declined to 63.2 % of the initial value. Following water washing, the flux hardly recovered, indicating a strong binding force between the dye molecules and the membrane surface. After two rounds of anti-fouling experiments, the flux of the TFC membrane still did not recover and had declined by 50.9 %. However, the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane showed excellent anti-fouling performance. In the first 24 h, the flux attenuation was 5.9 %. After two additional rounds of filtration experiments, although the TiO2 membrane experienced some flux attenuation, it was able to recover through UV irradiation.

By calculating the anti-pollution parameters of the periodic filtration experiment (Fig. 9 and Table S1&S2), it was found that the flux recovery rate of the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane (100 % for HA, 100 % for dye) was much higher than that of the TFC membrane (81 % for HA, 63 % for dye), whether for humic acid or dye. The total pollution of the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane was only 7.5 % and 23.6 % on average for HA and CR, and it was mainly reversible pollution. In contrast, the TFC membrane had a total average pollution rate of 26.4 % for HA, with almost all of it being irreversible. For the dye solution, the TFC membrane had an average pollution rate as high as 43.6 %, with irreversible pollution constituting the majority. Comparison with other self-cleaning nanofiltration membranes reveals that the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane prepared in this study demonstrates excellent self-cleaning performance, with a flux recovery rate exceeding that of most reports, as shown in Fig. 10 and Table S3. Moreover, the fundamental structure of TFC@TiO2/CNC remains intact, ensuring outstanding separation performance, and it holds significant potential for applications in areas such as wastewater treatment.

A method was developed for preparing a nanofiltration membrane by using vacuum filtration of a TiO2/CNC interlayer to assist the interfacial polymerization (IP) process. The prepared TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane exhibited a rough, hydrophilic, and negatively charged surface with a slightly larger pore size and a defect-free PA selective layer. The membrane achieved a permeance of 23.3 L/m2·h·bar, 95.5 % rejection of Na2SO4, 12.4 % rejection of NaCl, 98.4 % rejection of Congo red, and 95.8 % rejection of methyl blue. Additionally, the TFC@TiO2/CNC membrane demonstrated excellent anti-fouling performance, with over 99 % flux recovery ratio (FRR) and a total fouling ratio of 0.08 when filtering humic acid solution, and a 96 % FRR and a total fouling ratio of 0.24 when filtering dye solution. In summary, the photocatalytic membrane prepared by this method has significant application potential in the treatment of high-salinity wastewater and in anti-fouling membrane applications.
